Introduction

> In the contemporary digital landscape, mobile applications have become integral to personal and professional productivity. Among the various categories on the Google Play Store, the "Tools" category holds exceptional importance as it encompasses utility-driven software designed to simplify tasks, bridge communication gaps, and automate everyday workflows. Concurrently, Natural Language Processing (NLP) has emerged as a backbone for many of these modern tools, powering applications ranging from machine translation and AI keyboards to automated live transcription, voice dictation, and Optical Character Recognition (OCR).

As user bases scale into millions, the Google Play Store acts as a rich repository of consumer feedback. App reviews offer unvarnished insights into user experiences, feature demands, bugs, software stability, and overall sentiment. However, manually analyzing thousands of unstructured textual reviews to extract actionable insights is practically impossible.

This project addresses this challenge by performing an end-to-end Natural Language Processing and Topic Modeling analysis on user reviews scraped from 15 prominent NLP-driven utility applications within the Tools category (such as Google Translate, Microsoft Translator, Grammarly Keyboard, and Live Transcribe). By leveraging data scraping, linguistic preprocessing, sentiment classification, and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), this study extracts the hidden thematic structures governing user satisfaction and discontent, offering data-driven insights into how mobile tools can be iteratively improved to meet consumer expectations.
 
Objectives

The primary objective of this project is to implement an end-to-end computational framework to mine, process, and analyze consumer feedback for NLP-focused utility applications. The specific architectural objectives are defined as follows:

> Automated Data Scraping: Programmatically extract real-time textual reviews, user ratings, timestamps, and interaction metrics (such as thumbs-up counts) from 15 targeted NLP tool applications on the Google Play Store using web-scraping utilities.

> Text Normalization and Preprocessing: Establish a rigorous data cleaning pipeline utilizing Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) and langdetect to isolate English reviews, strip formatting noise (URLs, digits, punctuation, and emojis), and standardize text via tokenization, stop-word elimination, and Porter stemming.

> Sentiment Classification: Systematically map numerical star ratings into discrete sentiment classes (Positive, Neutral, and Negative) to quantify the macro-level user satisfaction rate across the selected application portfolio.

> Thematic Discovery via Topic Modeling: Apply Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to unmask dominant, hidden conversational themes within the global dataset and further dissect sentiment-specific topics to discover what specific features users praise versus what technical failures drive negative feedback.

> Interactive Visualization: Build an analytical dashboard interface to bridge the gap between complex algorithmic outputs and stakeholder interpretation.
 
Methodology and Text Pipeline

> The architecture of this system is organized into a modular pipeline, transitioning from raw data acquisition to statistical inference. The framework begins with Data Collection, using the google-play-scraper library to fetch localized English reviews directly from Google Play API servers. The acquired raw text is then routed through a Linguistic Preprocessing Engine. First, non-English reviews are filtered out dynamically using language detection algorithms. The remaining text undergoes case-folding to lowercase, followed by regular expression operations to strip out hyperlinks, digits, and a customized Unicode pattern targeting modern emojis.To prepare the structural matrices for mathematical models, the cleaned sentences are tokenized into word lists, standard English stop-words are pruned, and the words are reduced to their absolute morphological roots via the Porter Stemmer. For Thematic Clustering, a Document-Term Matrix (DTM) is formulated using a CountVectorizer constrained by document frequency boundaries to filter out words that are too rare or overly ubiquitous. This structured matrix is fed into a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model. To optimize structural stability, the model runs over multiple target topic counts ($k \in [2, 10]$) evaluating Griffith’s Log-Likelihood and model Perplexity. Finally, the text is split by localized sentiment ratings to map positive feedback dynamics (e.g., appreciation for translation accuracy or recording features) against negative product complaints (e.g., storage bugs, audio crashes, and device incompatibility issues).
 
 

NLP Analysis of Tools Category Apps Reviews from Google Play Store



This notebook performs an end-to-end analysis of Google Play Store reviews for NLP tools, covering data collection, preprocessing, topic modeling, and interactive visualization.
 
#### 1. Setup and Library Installation (1902e85c, ecabac85, 0c6e6924)

These initial cells ensure all necessary Python libraries (google-play-scraper, langdetect, nltk) are installed and their required data (like punkt_tab for NLTK) is downloaded. This sets up the environment for subsequent steps.

#### 2. Data Collection (aCt6pj1pF_3Z, _Pl_8JR3HBN6)

This section uses the google-play-scraper library to programmatically fetch reviews from a predefined list of NLP-related applications on the Google Play Store. It iterates through each app, retrieves a specified number of the newest reviews, and stores relevant information (app name, review content, rating, date, thumbs up count) into a Pandas DataFrame. Finally, it saves this collected data into a CSV file named nlp_tools_all_reviews.csv and provides an option to download it.
In [4]:
from google_play_scraper import reviews, Sort
import pandas as pd

# 15 NLP Tools Apps (with real Play Store IDs where possible)
apps = {
    "Google Translate": "com.google.android.apps.translate",
    "Microsoft Translator": "com.microsoft.translator",
    "Google Voice Typing": "com.google.android.googlequicksearchbox",
    "SpeechTexter": "spitcher.speechtexter",
    "Text Scanner OCR": "com.cv.docscanner",
    "Voice Notes AI": "com.google.android.keep",  # Google Keep (voice notes)
    "Grammarly Keyboard": "com.grammarly.android.keyboard",
    "AI Keyboard": "com.google.android.inputmethod.latin",  # Gboard
    "Live Transcribe": "com.google.audio.hearing.visualization.accessibility.scribe",
    "Google Text-to-Speech": "com.google.android.tts",
    "Speech-to-Text Converter": "com.google.android.apps.recorder",
    "AI Translator Apps": "com.deepl.mobiletranslator",
    "Photo-to-Text OCR": "com.adobe.scan.android",
    "Language Translator Offline": "com.vertaler.vertaler",
    "Smart Dictation": "com.google.android.apps.recorder"
}

# Store all reviews here
all_reviews = []

# Loop through all apps
for app_name, app_id in apps.items():
    try:
        result, _ = reviews(
            app_id,
            lang='en',
            country='us',
            sort=Sort.NEWEST,
            count=50  # number of reviews per app
        )

        for r in result:
            all_reviews.append({
                "app_name": app_name,
                "review": r["content"],
                "rating": r["score"],
                "date": r["at"],
                "thumbs_up": r["thumbsUpCount"]
            })

        print(f"Collected reviews for {app_name}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error for {app_name}: {e}")

# Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(all_reviews)

# Save to CSV
df.to_csv("nlp_tools_all_reviews.csv", index=False)

print(" All reviews saved successfully!")
Collected reviews for Google Translate Collected reviews for Microsoft Translator Collected reviews for Google Voice Typing Collected reviews for SpeechTexter Collected reviews for Text Scanner OCR Collected reviews for Voice Notes AI Collected reviews for Grammarly Keyboard Collected reviews for AI Keyboard Collected reviews for Live Transcribe Collected reviews for Google Text-to-Speech Collected reviews for Speech-to-Text Converter Collected reviews for AI Translator Apps Collected reviews for Photo-to-Text OCR Collected reviews for Language Translator Offline Collected reviews for Smart Dictation All reviews saved successfully!

#### 3.2 Post-Preprocessing Visualization

After the data preprocessing steps (cleaning, tokenization, stop-word removal), it's useful to visualize the changes and the resulting text data. This section will include visualizations to show the impact of preprocessing, such as a word cloud of cleaned reviews and potentially a comparison of review lengths.
 
#### 3. Data Preprocessing (LgU8IUNbHybH)

This crucial step cleans and prepares the raw review text for analysis. It involves several sub-steps:

- Loading Data: Reads the collected reviews from nlp_tools_all_reviews.csv.
- NLTK Downloads: Ensures stopwords and punkt tokenizer data are available.
- get_sentiment Function: Defines a helper function to categorize reviews into 'Negative', 'Neutral', or 'Positive' based on their star rating.
- is_english Function: Uses langdetect to filter out non-English reviews, ensuring the analysis focuses only on English content.
- remove_emojis Function: A utility to strip emoji characters from the text.
- preprocess_text Function: The core cleaning function that applies a series of transformations:
- Trimming spaces.
- Lowercasing text.
- Removing URLs, numbers, punctuation, and extra whitespaces.
- Applying tokenization, stop word removal, and stemming (using PorterStemmer).
- Application & Saving: Applies the is_english filter and preprocess_text function to create a new clean_review column. The processed data is then saved as cleaned_reviews.csv.
In [9]:
import pandas as pd
import re
import string

import nltk
nltk.download('stopwords')
nltk.download('punkt')

from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize
from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer

from langdetect import detect

# Load dataset
df = pd.read_csv("nlp_tools_all_reviews.csv")

stop_words = set(stopwords.words("english"))
stemmer = PorterStemmer()

# Function to assign sentiment based on rating
def get_sentiment(rating):
    if rating <= 2:
        return "Negative"
    elif rating == 3:
        return "Neutral"
    else:
        return "Positive"

# Function to check English language
def is_english(text):
    try:
        return detect(str(text)) == "en"
    except:
        return False

# Emoji remover
def remove_emojis(text):
    emoji_pattern = re.compile(
        "[\U0001F600-\U0001F64F"
        "\U0001F300-\U0001F5FF"
        "\U0001F680-\U0001F6FF"
        "\U0001F1E0-\U0001F1FF]+",
        flags=re.UNICODE
    )
    return emoji_pattern.sub(r'', text)

# Main cleaning function
def preprocess_text(text):

    text = str(text)

    # 1. Trim spaces
    text = text.strip()

    # 2. Lowercasing
    text = text.lower()

    # 3. Remove URLs
    text = re.sub(r'http\S+|www\S+', '', text)

    # 4. Remove numbers
    text = re.sub(r'\d+', '', text)

    # 5. Remove punctuation
    text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

    # 6. Remove emojis
    text = remove_emojis(text)

    # 7. Remove extra whitespaces
    text = " ".join(text.split())

    # 8. Tokenization
    words = word_tokenize(text)

    # 9. Remove stopwords
    words = [w for w in words if w not in stop_words]

    # 10. Stemming
    words = [stemmer.stem(w) for w in words]

    # 11. Join back text
    return " ".join(words)

# Apply language filtering (English only)
df = df[df["review"].apply(is_english)]

# Apply preprocessing
df["clean_review"] = df["review"].apply(preprocess_text)

# Save final dataset
df.to_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv", index=False)

print("Preprocessing completed successfully!")
print(df[["review", "clean_review"]].head())
[nltk_data] Downloading package stopwords to /root/nltk_data... [nltk_data] Unzipping corpora/stopwords.zip. [nltk_data] Downloading package punkt to /root/nltk_data... [nltk_data] Unzipping tokenizers/punkt.zip.
Preprocessing completed successfully! review \ 0 The best. 1 thankes 2 I aslo used after 9year 6 Unintelligible directions for occasional need ... 7 big company but perform waste speach one type ... clean_review 0 best 1 thank 2 aslo use year 6 unintellig direct occasion need foreign langua... 7 big compani perform wast speach one type trans...
 
#### 3.3 Review Length Comparison (Before and After Preprocessing)

This section visualizes the impact of the preprocessing steps on the length of the reviews. By comparing word counts before and after cleaning, we can observe the reduction in text size due to stop-word removal, punctuation stripping, and stemming.
In [10]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Load the raw and cleaned data
df_raw_for_len = pd.read_csv("nlp_tools_all_reviews.csv")
df_cleaned_for_len = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Calculate word counts for raw reviews
df_raw_for_len['raw_word_count'] = df_raw_for_len['review'].dropna().apply(lambda x: len(str(x).split()))

# Calculate word counts for cleaned reviews
df_cleaned_for_len['clean_word_count'] = df_cleaned_for_len['clean_review'].dropna().apply(lambda x: len(str(x).split()))

# Merge dataframes on a common identifier if available, or just take comparable lengths
# For simplicity, let's assume direct alignment for this visualization, or sample matching indices
# In a real scenario, you'd merge on 'review' content or index after filtering for English.
# For now, let's just use the first 'n' comparable rows.
min_len = min(len(df_raw_for_len), len(df_cleaned_for_len))
df_comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    'Raw Review Word Count': df_raw_for_len['raw_word_count'].head(min_len),
    'Cleaned Review Word Count': df_cleaned_for_len['clean_word_count'].head(min_len)
})

# Create a melted DataFrame for easier plotting with seaborn
df_melted = df_comparison.melt(var_name='Review Type', value_name='Word Count')

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.boxplot(x='Review Type', y='Word Count', data=df_melted, palette='Paired')
plt.title('Comparison of Review Lengths Before and After Preprocessing')
plt.xlabel('Review Type')
plt.ylabel('Word Count')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

#### 4.1 Post-Stemming Visualization

After applying stemming, we can visualize the most frequent words in their root forms to see the effect of this normalization step. A word cloud of the stemmed user reviews (loaded from stemmed_reviews.csv) will highlight the core terms remaining after linguistic reduction.
 
#### 4. Stemming (CdcQI9TJKGlh)

This cell specifically demonstrates and applies stemming using the PorterStemmer from NLTK. It takes the clean_review column and further reduces words to their root form, storing the result in a new stemmed_review column. This step is part of text normalization to reduce vocabulary size and improve topic modeling accuracy.
In [12]:
import pandas as pd
from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer
from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize

stemmer = PorterStemmer()

def stemming(text):
    words = word_tokenize(text)
    stemmed_words = [stemmer.stem(word) for word in words]
    return " ".join(stemmed_words)

# Load the cleaned reviews
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Apply stemming
df["stemmed_review"] = df["clean_review"].apply(stemming)

# Save the DataFrame with the new stemmed_review column
df.to_csv("stemmed_reviews.csv", index=False)

print(df[["clean_review", "stemmed_review"]].head())
print("Stemmed reviews saved to stemmed_reviews.csv")
clean_review \ 0 best 1 thank 2 aslo use year 3 unintellig direct occasion need foreign langua... 4 big compani perform wast speach one type trans... stemmed_review 0 best 1 thank 2 aslo use year 3 unintellig direct occas need foreign languag t... 4 big compani perform wast speach one type trans... Stemmed reviews saved to stemmed_reviews.csv

#### 6. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) for General Topics (hryERMCOLKLl)

This section performs topic modeling on all cleaned reviews. It loads the cleaned_reviews.csv, converts the text into a document-term matrix using CountVectorizer (removing common English stop words), and then applies LatentDirichletAllocation to extract a specified number of topics (here, 5). It then prints the top 10 words for each identified topic, providing an initial understanding of the themes present in the reviews. Finally, it assigns the most dominant topic to each review and saves the result as lda_topics_reviews.csv.
In [15]:
# This cell is redundant as pyLDAvis installation is handled in QdTuMJ5WVRRo.
In [16]:
import pandas as pd
import nltk

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# nltk.download('punkt') # Removed: Already downloaded in LgU8IUNbHybH
# nltk.download('stopwords') # Removed: Already downloaded in LgU8IUNbHybH

# Load cleaned dataset
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Drop missing values
texts = df["clean_review"].dropna().astype(str)

# Convert text into document-term matrix
vectorizer = CountVectorizer(
    max_df=0.95,
    min_df=2,
    stop_words='english'
)

dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)

# Apply LDA model
lda_model = LatentDirichletAllocation(
    n_components=5,   # number of topics
    random_state=42
)

lda_model.fit(dtm)

# Print Topics
words = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

print("\n===== TOPIC MODELING RESULTS =====")

for i, topic in enumerate(lda_model.components_):
    top_words = [words[i] for i in topic.argsort()[-10:]]
    print(f"\n Topic {i+1}: {top_words}")

# Assign topic to each review
topic_values = lda_model.transform(dtm)
df = df.iloc[texts.index]  # align index properly
df["topic"] = topic_values.argmax(axis=1)

# Save final file
df.to_csv("lda_topics_reviews.csv", index=False)

print("\n LDA completed and file saved as 'lda_topics_reviews.csv'")
===== TOPIC MODELING RESULTS ===== Topic 1: ['better', 'pixel', 'translat', 'work', 'help', 'use', 'record', 'googl', 'good', 'app'] Topic 2: ['like', 'recent', 'spanish', 'option', 'app', 'star', 'translat', 'edit', 'use', 'note'] Topic 3: ['best', 'phone', 'translat', 'love', 'thank', 'great', 'want', 'dont', 'need', 'app'] Topic 4: ['dont', 'lot', 'file', 'time', 'save', 'audio', 'option', 'app', 'record', 'use'] Topic 5: ['chines', 'like', 'im', 'sound', 'languag', 'great', 'word', 'use', 'app', 'translat'] LDA completed and file saved as 'lda_topics_reviews.csv'
 
#### 6.1 General Topic Distribution Visualization

After applying Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to identify general topics, this visualization shows the distribution of reviews across these topics. This helps to understand which themes are most prevalent in the overall dataset.
In [17]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Load dataset with topics assigned
df_topics = pd.read_csv("lda_topics_reviews.csv")

# Visualize the distribution of general topics
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='topic', data=df_topics, palette='Set2')
plt.title('Distribution of Reviews Across General LDA Topics')
plt.xlabel('Topic Number')
plt.ylabel('Number of Reviews')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()
/tmp/ipykernel_9171/1981329319.py:10: FutureWarning: Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect. sns.countplot(x='topic', data=df_topics, palette='Set2')
 
#### 7. Evaluating the Number of Topics (qMNYWQrKLjPy)

This code block helps determine the optimal number of topics for the LDA model. It iterates through a range of possible topic counts (from 2 to 10), trains an LDA model for each, and calculates two metrics: perplexity (lower is better) and log-likelihood (used as an approximation for coherence, higher is better). It then suggests the number of topics that yielded the highest log-likelihood score as the 'best' number of topics.
In [18]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")
texts = df["clean_review"].dropna().astype(str)

# Convert text into matrix
vectorizer = CountVectorizer(max_df=0.95, min_df=2, stop_words='english')
dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)

# Range of topics to test
topic_range = range(2, 11)

coherence_scores = []
perplexity_scores = []

print("\n===== Evaluating Topic Numbers =====")

for n in topic_range:

    lda = LatentDirichletAllocation(
        n_components=n,
        random_state=42
    )

    lda.fit(dtm)

    # Perplexity (lower is better)
    perplexity = lda.perplexity(dtm)

    # Approximation for coherence (sklearn does not give all metrics directly)
    score = lda.score(dtm)

    coherence_scores.append(score)
    perplexity_scores.append(perplexity)

    print(f"Topics: {n} | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): {score:.2f} | Perplexity: {perplexity:.2f}")

# Find best topic count (simple rule)
best_index = np.argmax(coherence_scores)
best_topics = list(topic_range)[best_index]

print("\n===================================")
print(f" Best number of topics: {best_topics}")
print("===================================")
===== Evaluating Topic Numbers ===== Topics: 2 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -19346.12 | Perplexity: 389.75 Topics: 3 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -19589.51 | Perplexity: 420.12 Topics: 4 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -19728.88 | Perplexity: 438.57 Topics: 5 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -19867.82 | Perplexity: 457.77 Topics: 6 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -19990.32 | Perplexity: 475.39 Topics: 7 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -20094.74 | Perplexity: 490.95 Topics: 8 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -20044.14 | Perplexity: 483.35 Topics: 9 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -20110.03 | Perplexity: 493.27 Topics: 10 | Log Likelihood (Griffiths): -20175.51 | Perplexity: 503.33 =================================== Best number of topics: 2 ===================================
 
#### 8. Sentiment-Specific LDA (AF4aquqgL45I)

Building upon the general LDA, this section performs topic modeling separately for positive and negative reviews. It first loads the cleaned_reviews.csv and re-applies the get_sentiment function. Then, it filters the DataFrame into positive_texts and negative_texts. For each sentiment group, it applies CountVectorizer and LatentDirichletAllocation (with 3 components each) to identify distinct topics relevant to either positive or negative feedback. The top 10 words for these sentiment-specific topics are printed.
In [19]:
# This cell is redundant as LDA visualization is handled in QdTuMJ5WVRRo.
In [20]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# Load dataset (must contain sentiment + clean_review)
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Apply function to create 'sentiment' column (assuming get_sentiment is defined in an earlier cell)
df["sentiment"] = df["rating"].apply(get_sentiment)

# 🧹 Keep only required columns and drop missing values after sentiment is assigned
df = df.dropna(subset=["clean_review", "sentiment"])

# =========================
# POSITIVE REVIEWS LDA
# =========================
positive_texts = df[df["sentiment"] == "Positive"]["clean_review"]

vectorizer_p = CountVectorizer(max_df=0.95, min_df=2, stop_words='english')
dtm_p = vectorizer_p.fit_transform(positive_texts)

lda_p = LatentDirichletAllocation(n_components=3, random_state=42)
lda_p.fit(dtm_p)

print("\n===== POSITIVE TOPICS =====")
words_p = vectorizer_p.get_feature_names_out()

for i, topic in enumerate(lda_p.components_):
    print(f"\nTopic {i+1}:")
    print([words_p[i] for i in topic.argsort()[-10:]])

# =========================
#  NEGATIVE REVIEWS LDA
# =========================
negative_texts = df[df["sentiment"] == "Negative"]["clean_review"]

vectorizer_n = CountVectorizer(max_df=0.95, min_df=2, stop_words='english')
dtm_n = vectorizer_n.fit_transform(negative_texts)

lda_n = LatentDirichletAllocation(n_components=3, random_state=42)
lda_n.fit(dtm_n)

print("\n===== NEGATIVE TOPICS =====")
words_n = vectorizer_n.get_feature_names_out()

for i, topic in enumerate(lda_n.components_):
    print(f"\nTopic {i+1}:")
    print([words_n[i] for i in topic.argsort()[-10:]])
===== POSITIVE TOPICS ===== Topic 1: ['googl', 'lot', 'best', 'help', 'love', 'translat', 'great', 'use', 'good', 'app'] Topic 2: ['need', 'im', 'like', 'better', 'help', 'easi', 'use', 'thank', 'translat', 'app'] Topic 3: ['reliabl', 'delay', 'dictat', 'transcript', 'time', 'audio', 'simpl', 'thing', 'work', 'record'] ===== NEGATIVE TOPICS ===== Topic 1: ['chang', 'save', 'use', 'folder', 'doesnt', 'file', 'edit', 'option', 'app', 'record'] Topic 2: ['work', 'creat', 'word', 'delet', 'record', 'need', 'googl', 'use', 'translat', 'app'] Topic 3: ['audio', 'ad', 'wast', 'pixel', 'uninstal', 'want', 'use', 'time', 'dont', 'phone']
 
#### 9. Saving Sentiment-Specific Topics (XhmTdnCqTkEC, bKC1AdaqTyPu)

This cell consolidates the topic words discovered in the sentiment-specific LDA (from cell AF4aquqgL45I) into a text file named topics_output.txt. It writes out the top words for both positive and negative topics, making it easy to review and interpret them. The file is then made available for download.

In [22]:
import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Function to assign sentiment based on rating
def get_sentiment(rating):
    if rating <= 2:
        return "Negative"
    elif rating == 3:
        return "Neutral"
    else:
        return "Positive"

# Apply function to create 'sentiment' column
df["sentiment"] = df["rating"].apply(get_sentiment)

# 🧹 Drop missing values after sentiment is assigned
df = df.dropna(subset=["clean_review", "sentiment"])

st.title("NLP Play Store Reviews Analysis")

# ======================
# FILTER SENTIMENT
# ======================
sentiment_choice = st.selectbox("Select Sentiment", ["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"])

filtered_df = df[df["sentiment"] == sentiment_choice]

st.write("### Sample Reviews")
st.write(filtered_df[["clean_review", "sentiment"]].head())

# ======================
# TOPIC MODELING
# ======================
st.write("### Topic Modeling Results")

texts = filtered_df["clean_review"].dropna()

# Check if there are enough texts for topic modeling
if len(texts) > 0:
    vectorizer = CountVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_df=0.95, min_df=2)
    dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)

    lda = LatentDirichletAllocation(n_components=3, random_state=42)
    lda.fit(dtm)

    words = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

    for i, topic in enumerate(lda.components_):
        st.write(f"Topic {i+1}:")
        st.write([words[i] for i in topic.argsort()[-10:]])
else:
    st.write("No reviews found for the selected sentiment to perform topic modeling.")

In [23]:
%%writefile app.py
import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Function to assign sentiment based on rating
def get_sentiment(rating):
    if rating <= 2:
        return "Negative"
    elif rating == 3:
        return "Neutral"
    else:
        return "Positive"

# Apply function to create 'sentiment' column
df["sentiment"] = df["rating"].apply(get_sentiment)

# Drop missing values after sentiment is assigned
df = df.dropna(subset=["clean_review", "sentiment"])

st.title("NLP Play Store Reviews Analysis")

# ======================
# FILTER SENTIMENT
# ======================
sentiment_choice = st.selectbox("Select Sentiment", ["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"])

filtered_df = df[df["sentiment"] == sentiment_choice]

st.write("### Sample Reviews")
st.write(filtered_df[["clean_review", "sentiment"]].head())

# ======================
# TOPIC MODELING
# ======================
st.write("### Topic Modeling Results")

texts = filtered_df["clean_review"].dropna()

# Check if there are enough texts for topic modeling
if len(texts) > 0:
    vectorizer = CountVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_df=0.95, min_df=2)
    dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)

    lda = LatentDirichletAllocation(n_components=3, random_state=42)
    lda.fit(dtm)

    words = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

    for i, topic in enumerate(lda.components_):
        st.write(f"Topic {i+1}:")
        st.write([words[i] for i in topic.argsort()[-10:]])
else:
    st.write("No reviews found for the selected sentiment to perform topic modeling.")
Writing app.py

#### 11. pyLDAvis Visualization (QdTuMJ5WVRRo, 5GP7Lh0OV4QL, V1UG10nYXCA-)

This section generates an interactive visualization of the LDA topic model using pyLDAvis.

- QdTuMJ5WVRRo: Installs pyLDAvis, loads cleaned_reviews.csv, takes a sample of reviews, converts them into a document-term matrix, and trains an LDA model (with 3 topics). It then prepares the data for pyLDAvis and saves the interactive visualization as lda_visualization.html, which is then downloaded.
- 5GP7Lh0OV4QL: Displays the generated pyLDAvis interactive panel directly within the notebook.
- V1UG10nYXCA-: Enables notebook integration for pyLDAvis and outputs the panel object, which is the prepared visualization data.
In [ ]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

# Function to assign sentiment based on rating
def get_sentiment(rating):
    if rating <= 2:
        return "Negative"
    elif rating == 3:
        return "Neutral"
    else:
        return "Positive"

# Load dataset
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Apply function to create 'sentiment' column
df["sentiment"] = df["rating"].apply(get_sentiment)

# Remove empty rows
df = df.dropna(subset=["clean_review"])

# Use smaller sample for speed
sample_size = 1000
df = df.sample(min(sample_size, len(df)), random_state=42)

# ======================
# POSITIVE REVIEWS
# ======================
positive_reviews = df[df["sentiment"] == "Positive"]["clean_review"].dropna()

vectorizer_p = CountVectorizer(
    stop_words='english',
    max_features=500
)

dtm_p = vectorizer_p.fit_transform(positive_reviews)

lda_p = LatentDirichletAllocation(
    n_components=2,
    max_iter=3,
    random_state=42
)

lda_p.fit(dtm_p)

words_p = vectorizer_p.get_feature_names_out()

# ======================
# NEGATIVE REVIEWS
# ======================
negative_reviews = df[df["sentiment"] == "Negative"]["clean_review"].dropna()

vectorizer_n = CountVectorizer(
    stop_words='english',
    max_features=500
)

dtm_n = vectorizer_n.fit_transform(negative_reviews)

lda_n = LatentDirichletAllocation(
    n_components=2,
    max_iter=3,
    random_state=42
)

lda_n.fit(dtm_n)

words_n = vectorizer_n.get_feature_names_out()

# ======================
# SAVE TOPICS
# ======================
with open("topics_output.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:

    f.write("===== POSITIVE TOPICS =====\n")

    for i, topic in enumerate(lda_p.components_):
        topic_words = [words_p[j] for j in topic.argsort()[-10:]]
        f.write(f"\nTopic {i+1}: {topic_words}\n")

    f.write("\n===== NEGATIVE TOPICS =====\n")

    for i, topic in enumerate(lda_n.components_):
        topic_words = [words_n[j] for j in topic.argsort()[-10:]]
        f.write(f"\nTopic {i+1}: {topic_words}\n")

print("Topics saved successfully!")
 
#### 10. Streamlit Interactive Application (169dcf68, hDmZvAm5NrC8, 815223db, ZE2-mne6OA5s)

This series of cells creates an interactive web application using Streamlit to explore the reviews and topic models dynamically.

- 169dcf68: Installs the streamlit library.
- hDmZvAm5NrC8: Defines the Streamlit application logic. It loads the cleaned_reviews.csv, assigns sentiment, and then creates a user interface where one can select a sentiment ('Positive', 'Negative', 'Neutral'). Based on the selection, it displays sample reviews and performs a new LDA topic modeling specific to the filtered sentiment, showing the top words for the topics. This allows for interactive exploration of sentiment-driven themes.
- 815223db: Writes the Streamlit application code to a file named app.py.
- ZE2-mne6OA5s: Runs the Streamlit application using !streamlit run app.py, making it accessible via a local URL.
In [ ]:
from google.colab import files
files.download("topics_output.txt")
 

Detailed Interpretation of Sentiment-Specific Topics



Based on the sentiment-specific Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) models:

Positive Topics revealed key aspects users appreciate:

* App Utility and Helpfulness: Reviews frequently praise apps that are genuinely useful and provide significant assistance, often using terms like 'great', 'help', 'app', 'use', 'thank', 'need', and 'document'. This indicates a strong positive sentiment towards tools that efficiently solve user problems.
* General Positive Experience & Core Functionality: This topic highlights satisfaction with the overall effectiveness and core features of the apps, particularly translation apps. Words like 'work', 'great', 'love', 'googl', 'help', 'use', 'best', 'good', and 'translat' suggest users value reliable and high-quality performance.
* Quality and Specific Features: Positive reviews also touch upon the quality of the app, including aspects like 'good', 'experi', 'record', 'applic', 'thing', 'use', 'free', 'like', and 'star'. This could indicate appreciation for good quality features, including recording capabilities, and a general positive experience.

Negative Topics pinpoint critical areas of user dissatisfaction:

* Feature and Organizational Issues: A common theme in negative reviews revolves around problems with app features, settings, and organization. Terms such as 'need', 'translat', 'set', 'edit', 'featur', 'creat', 'folder', 'googl', 'record', and 'app' suggest user frustration with complex configurations, missing features, or difficulty managing content like folders or recorded items, especially concerning translation.
* Resource Consumption and Performance: This topic points to issues related to data usage, audio problems, phone performance impact, and time wastage. Keywords like 'wast', 'need', 'data', 'audio', 'phone', 'record', 'dont', 'time', 'app', and 'use' indicate that users are displeased with apps consuming excessive resources, having unreliable audio functions, or leading to an inefficient experience.
* Uninstall Difficulties and Device-Specific Problems: Negative feedback also highlights difficulties with app removal, unwanted features, and issues specific to certain devices. Terms like 'uninstal', 'phone', 'option', 'delet', 'use', 'record', 'want', 'pixel', 'app', and 'translat' suggest frustration with the inability to easily uninstall apps, device-specific compatibility issues (e.g., with Pixel phones), or unwanted recording and translation functionalities.
 
#### 11. pyLDAvis Visualization (QdTuMJ5WVRRo, 5GP7Lh0OV4QL, V1UG10nYXCA-)

This section generates an interactive visualization of the LDA topic model using pyLDAvis.

- QdTuMJ5WVRRo: Installs pyLDAvis, loads cleaned_reviews.csv, takes a sample of reviews, converts them into a document-term matrix, and trains an LDA model (with 3 topics). It then prepares the data for pyLDAvis and saves the interactive visualization as lda_visualization.html, which is then downloaded.
- 5GP7Lh0OV4QL: Displays the generated pyLDAvis interactive panel directly within the notebook.
- V1UG10nYXCA-: Enables notebook integration for pyLDAvis and outputs the panel object, which is the prepared visualization data.
In [ ]:
# ============================================
# COMPLETE LDA + GRAPH VISUALIZATION + SAVE
# ============================================

# Install pyLDAvis
!pip install pyLDAvis -q

# ======================
# IMPORT LIBRARIES
# ======================
import pandas as pd
import pyLDAvis
import pyLDAvis.lda_model

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation

from google.colab import files

# ======================
# LOAD DATASET
# ======================
df = pd.read_csv("cleaned_reviews.csv")

# Remove empty rows
df = df.dropna(subset=["clean_review"])

# Use smaller sample for fast execution
sample_size = min(1000, len(df)) # Take at most 1000 rows or all rows if less than 1000
df = df.sample(sample_size, random_state=42)

texts = df["clean_review"]

# ======================
# TEXT TO NUMERIC MATRIX
# ======================
vectorizer = CountVectorizer(
    stop_words='english',
    max_features=500
)

dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(texts)

# ======================
# APPLY LDA MODEL
# ======================
lda_model = LatentDirichletAllocation(
    n_components=3,
    max_iter=5,
    random_state=42
)

lda_model.fit(dtm)

# ======================
# PRINT TOPICS
# ======================
words = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

print("\n===== LDA TOPICS =====")

for i, topic in enumerate(lda_model.components_):
    top_words = [words[j] for j in topic.argsort()[-10:]]
    print(f"\n📌 Topic {i+1}:")
    print(top_words)

# ======================
# CREATE VISUALIZATION
# ======================
panel = pyLDAvis.lda_model.prepare(
    lda_model,
    dtm,
    vectorizer
)

# ======================
# DISPLAY GRAPH
# ======================
pyLDAvis.display(panel)

# ======================
# SAVE HTML FILE
# ======================
pyLDAvis.save_html(panel, "lda_visualization.html")

print("\n✅ LDA Graph saved successfully!")

# ======================
# DOWNLOAD HTML FILE
# ======================
files.download("lda_visualization.html")
In [ ]:
from IPython.display import display
display(panel)
In [ ]:

            

        

        

            
In [ ]:

            

                
import pyLDAvis; pyLDAvis.enable_notebook(); panel

            

        

        

            
 

            

                
Interpretation of the LDA Visualization (pyLDAvis)


The pyLDAvis visualization is an interactive tool that helps understand the topics identified by Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and the terms associated with them. It consists of two main parts:

1.  Intertopic Distance Map (Left Panel):
    *   Circles: Each circle represents a topic. The size of the circle is proportional to the overall prevalence of that topic in the corpus (how many documents are associated with it).
    *   Distance: The distance between the centers of the circles indicates how similar or dissimilar the topics are. Topics that are closer together are more similar in terms of their word distributions, while those further apart are more distinct.
    *   Overlap: Overlapping circles might suggest that those topics share a significant number of words or cover related themes.

2.  Bar Chart of Top-Salient Terms (Right Panel):
    *   When you select a topic circle on the left, the bar chart on the right updates to show the most salient terms for that specific topic. Salient terms are those that are both frequent and distinctive to that topic.
    *   Overall Term Frequency (Red Bars): Shows how frequently a term appears in the entire dataset.
    *   Estimated Term Frequency within Selected Topic (Blue Bars): Shows how frequently a term appears within the currently selected topic.
    *   Relevance Slider (λ): You can adjust the λ (lambda) slider to change the relevance of terms.
        *   A λ closer to 1 (default) emphasizes terms that are unique and highly characteristic of the selected topic, even if they are not very frequent overall.
        *   A λ closer to 0 emphasizes terms that are very frequent within the topic, even if they are also frequent in other topics.

            

        

        

            
 

            

                
#Conclusion and Future Work

This analysis has provided valuable insights into user sentiment and the underlying topics in Google Play Store reviews for NLP tools. We successfully collected, cleaned, and preprocessed review data, applied both general and sentiment-specific topic modeling using LDA, and developed an interactive Streamlit application and pyLDAvis visualization for exploration.

Key Findings:

*   Positive sentiment is strongly associated with app utility, core functionality, and quality of specific features like recording and translation.
*   Negative sentiment is driven by frustrations with feature implementation, resource consumption (data, audio), and technical issues such as uninstall difficulties or device-specific bugs.
*   The optimal number of topics for the general LDA model was determined to be 2, indicating broad categories of discussion within the reviews.

Applications:

The insights derived from this analysis have several practical applications for app developers, product managers, and marketing teams:

1.  Product Improvement: Developers can prioritize features and bug fixes based on the most prevalent negative topics (e.g., resource consumption, uninstall issues, specific device compatibility like Pixel). Positive topics can guide the enhancement of successful features.
2.  Marketing & Messaging: Marketing teams can craft more effective campaigns by highlighting features that resonate with positive user experiences (e.g., app utility, high-quality translation) and addressing common pain points identified in negative reviews.
3.  Competitive Analysis: By extending this analysis to competitors' apps, product managers can identify areas where their app excels or falls short, informing strategic decisions and differentiation efforts.
4.  Customer Support: Understanding common negative topics can help customer support teams anticipate user issues, develop targeted FAQs, and improve response strategies.
5.  New Feature Development: Identifying unmet needs or emerging trends from the topics can inspire the development of new features that directly address user demands or resolve persistent problems.
6.  User Experience (UX) Design: UX designers can leverage insights into usability challenges (e.g., feature organization, setting configurations) to create more intuitive and user-friendly interfaces.

Future Work:

To further enhance this analysis, consider the following:

1.  Time-Series Analysis of Topics: Analyze how topic prevalence and sentiment change over time to identify trends or the impact of app updates.
2.  Advanced Sentiment Analysis: Implement more sophisticated sentiment analysis techniques (e.g., deep learning models) to capture nuanced emotions beyond simple positive/negative/neutral.
3.  Topic Coherence Evaluation: Utilize more robust topic coherence metrics (e.g., C_V, UMass) for a more definitive selection of the optimal number of topics.
4.  Named Entity Recognition (NER): Extract specific entities (e.g., app features, brand names, devices) from reviews to gain granular insights.
5.  Comparative Analysis: Extend the analysis to compare reviews across different NLP app categories or competitor apps to identify unique selling propositions or common pain points.
6.  User Interface Enhancements: Further develop the Streamlit application to include more interactive charts, filters, and drill-down capabilities for deeper exploration of topics and reviews.
